text: Playa Paraíso. But I chose to stay behind at the cabana, to be alone and meditate. I sat quietly on a hammock meditating. Softly, sea waves stroked the shore. The whole atmosphere was sublime. When, out of the blue, a jaguar stepped into my mind’s eye! (Understand, I was wide awake, with my eyes closed, and yet I was suddenly dreaming vividly.) A large jaguar from my right flank lay down in front of me, with a cat’s air of nonchalance. Although something extraordinary was happening, I continued to observe quietly and follow the breath. I became aware of a white pyramid in the distance. It was flanked by two human figures in the foreground. It seemed that these two were also very intent upon this jaguar. This lasted several minutes, and then suddenly a snake rose up, right in my face. She (for I knew somehow that the snake was female) looked beautiful and wise, yet dangerous; she was sentient, venerable, and immeasurably exquisite. I knew instinctively the serpent was here to swallow me. I was thrilled. I knew I was having an honest-to-God vision. I loved everything about this snake. As I went into her mouth, throat, and neck, swallowed whole, I felt no fear, only thrilling love. Surrendering to her beauty, her wisdom, everything triggered unconditional love. In the darkness of her belly, I thought, as I was dissolving: “love you, love you, love you.” Trusting the process, I dissolved into oblivion. Simultaneously, I was on the beach, wide awake, meditating. I’d passed the final test review of some alchemical mystery play, choreographed in my mind’s eye. Ecstatic wonder took me because of this visionary phenomenon; sitting wide awake, with a full-on hypnogogic dream, I stayed planted, meditating. Minutes later, another vision occurred: I was under the sea, heading east over the reefs. There were two guides, like dolphins, behind me, one on each side. We were heading out, perpendicular to the shore, out to the continental shelf. From there, straight down to the ocean’s abyssal plain, then farther down, into utter darkness and oblivion. Meanwhile, on the beach, I was listening to the waves. And then it struck me that I was right between North and South America, and this seemed to me to be like the two hemispheres of the brain. Central America was like the corpus callosum and the Yucatan was the thalamus or the pineal gland (which help regulate waking and dreaming). The reefs I’d just crossed resembled sulci along the neocortex; the continental shelf was like one side of the fissure of commissure; the abyssal plain was comparable to the dense corpus callosum that spreads below the fissure of commissure. The structures in my brain were being out-pictured as the landscape in this hypnogogic “vision,” while the earth’s geography seemed like a simulacra of the brain’s physiology. The sensation of one’s internal physiology getting projected as the outer landscape is explored in a seminal book, The Body of Myth , by J. Nigro Sansonese, in which yogic meditation practices, proprioception, mythology, and visionary trances are examined at length and in detail. I intuited that other features of the earth’s geography were of a psychological nature: the Yucatan jungle, the Mayan jungle, corresponded with the conscious (rational) world of the senses, or maya . The sea corresponded to the moist unconscious, the magic realm, where dreamtime and archetypal imagination coexist, and where great forces in the spirit occasionally emerge from behind veils, darkness, and the uncharted depths of our ignorance. The shoreline was a liminal territory, where the creatures of the sea sometimes meet the creatures of the land. But the shore was also a primeval boundary where the conscious and the unconscious minds carry on a relationship between their different levels of awareness, linking our perceptions and our feelings, and the waking and dreaming states of consciousness. It’s feasible that the unconscious realm, which we experience in our dreams and in hypnogogic visions, was, once upon a time, not so unconscious. Nowadays, our dreams tend to fade quickly and we generally disregard them. Insofar as the rational mind dominates our waking consciousness, we remain unconscious of the soul’s capacity for clairvoyant hypnogogic dreaming. Recovering the soul’s capacity for hypnogogic dreaming may involve awakening the heart mind. The heart mind is associated with the limbic brain, the emotional cortex. Dreams may be vestiges of an archaic faculty that’s generally dormant now, except during REM sleep. Consider that the pineal gland is a vestigial eyeball, sunk deep in the core of our brains, sheltered from direct light. The pineal gland evolved in fish for perceiving light in the outer world; now, in humans, the pineal gland is a light-transducing organ, it can produce molecules that create incredible visions. The heart mind uses symbols to communicate with the rational mind across the intelligence divide. The rational mind has so dominated our lives that the heart mind recedes into the unconscious, because our survival depends on a sensory viewpoint and rational thought. So the heart-mind, the feeling center, sinks into the dark, watery unconscious, until it’s awakened by circumstances. Death is the great awakener. There are many circumstances that awaken the heart-mind and bring on visionary states; a heart-rending loss is only one catalyst among many emotional states that can awaken the mind’s eye, revealing the otherworld, making it seem tangible. Bent so low by death and separation, the otherworld opens on hinges in the heart. The mythical figure carved on the Temple of the Descending God at the ancient ruins of Tulum is an inverted person, with his legs above and arms below, also known as the “Diving God.” Above this figure appear two plumed serpents, which might symbolize the eclipse cycle, with its “dragon nodes” tracking the cosmic intercourse of the sun and moon; or they might depict the human kundalini circuit, which consists of twin serpents called Ida and Pingala; or they could represent the universal tension of opposites, which produces friction and
